frontiers_requirements_checked.main_figures: TIF/TIFF or JPEG; RGB; 300 dpi at final size

examples: Figure 1. Study architecture and claim-boundary map. (A) Evidence hierarchy from public bulk RNA recurrence, continuous case-only immune spectrum, residual co-event remodeling, paired COMBAT RNA-CyTOF bridging, donor-level single-cell context and exploratory Death28 anchoring. (B) Locked interpretation: inflammatory/MHC-II decoupling is associated with a prespecified residual CD3/CD14 co-event signal in paired sepsis samples. (C) Claim boundaries separating allowed cross-cohort, paired, activation-state and computational interpretations from excluded causal mechanism, physical CD3/CD14 complex, validated endotype, clinical prediction and therapeutic claims.

Background: Whole-blood RNA sequencing and cytometry assays capture different immune analytical layers, which can obscure how inflammation and immunoparalysis coexist in sepsis. Methods: We integrated seven public bulk sepsis transcriptomic cohorts, pre-analyzed cytometry summaries, COMBAT paired RNA sequencing and mass cytometry (CyTOF) participant-timepoint data, matched raw-FCS sensitivity analyses, and donor-level single-cell contextual analysis. Results: A total of 660 bulk RNA samples (499 sepsis cases) were included. The prespecified six-gene host-response panel showed inverse coupling with MHC-II/CD74 across all seven cohorts (median Spearman ρ approximately -0.60), and broad-cell composition sensitivity attenuated but did not reverse the primary direction. Case-only analysis supported a continuous immune-decoupling gradient rather than a validated clinical endotype. In COMBAT Sepsis-only paired samples (40 rows from 34 participants), residual CD3/CD14 co-event signal was negatively associated with HLA-DR core RNA score (ρ = -0.559, permutation p = 4.00 × 10⁻⁴) and MHC-II/CD74 RNA score (ρ = -0.517, permutation p = 0.0011), and positively associated with RNA decoupling index (ρ = 0.462, permutation p = 0.0035). Row-level, participant-level, participant-cluster, and broad-cell composition-adjusted sensitivities preserved association directions with expected attenuation. Raw-FCS all-pair analysis did not resolve CD3/CD14 pair specificity; CD3/CD14 is retained as a prespecified representative residual co-event signal. Conclusion: These findings support a bounded systems immunology reconstruction of sepsis-associated immunoparalysis that requires prospective paired biological validation.

Figure 3. Continuous-spectrum case-only immune decoupling and pathway context. (A) Case-only immune-decoupling spectrum in 499 sepsis cases, with k=3 shown as a descriptive discretization of a continuous PC1 gradient. (B) PC1 alignment with host-response, myeloid, immunometabolic, MHC-II/CD74, HLA-DR, interferon/antigen-presentation and adaptive/T-cell programs. (C) Cross-cohort centroid-transfer stability trained from the largest cohort. (D) Ranked pathway context from recoverable public cohorts. These results do not establish validated clinical endotypes or causal pathway effects.

Sepsis is characterized by the paradoxical coexistence of robust inflammatory activation and impaired antigen presentation capacity, which collectively disrupt coordinated immune responses. This complex immunological state remains challenging to investigate because whole-blood transcriptomics and cytometry assays interrogate distinct analytical layers. The present study integrates public RNA sequencing and mass cytometry datasets to systematically examine this problem across multiple analytical layers. Across seven independent public sepsis transcriptomic cohorts, inflammatory gene expression programs consistently exhibited inverse correlation with MHC-II and HLA-DR-associated transcriptional signatures. In the COMBAT dataset containing paired RNA and CyTOF measurements, CD3/CD14 co-event abundance was significantly associated with reduced MHC-II/HLA-DR transcriptomic scores in sepsis samples. Critically, the residual CD3/CD14 co-event signal demonstrated activation-state remodeling rather than uniform HLA-DR loss. The incremental contribution is not rediscovery of sepsis-associated MHC-II/HLA-DR suppression, but a paired public RNA-CyTOF cross-layer linkage between systemic RNA decoupling and a residual CD3/CD14 co-event signal in matched sepsis samples. The expanded analyses further frame the case-only groups as a continuous immune-decoupling gradient and show that CD3/CD14 should be interpreted as a representative co-event signal after broader marker-pair specificity hardening, not as a uniquely specific structural interaction. This work reframes sepsis-associated immunoparalysis as a compartment-aware systems immunology problem, while explicitly avoiding claims of causality, structural interaction formation, therapeutic implications, and clinical prediction modeling.

This was a public-data systems immunology study designed for submission to Frontiers in Immunology, Systems Immunology section, as an Original Research article. No MIMIC-IV, eICU, protected health information, new human samples, wet-laboratory experiments, MR/SMR causal analysis, clinical prediction modeling, drug repositioning, or treatment-response modeling was performed.

Figure 1. Study architecture and claim-boundary map. (A) Evidence hierarchy from public bulk RNA recurrence, continuous case-only immune spectrum, residual co-event remodeling, paired COMBAT RNA-CyTOF bridging, donor-level single-cell context and exploratory Death28 anchoring. (B) Locked interpretation: inflammatory/MHC-II decoupling is associated with a prespecified residual CD3/CD14 co-event signal in paired sepsis samples. (C) Claim boundaries separating allowed cross-cohort, paired, activation-state and computational interpretations from excluded causal mechanism, physical CD3/CD14 complex, validated endotype, clinical prediction and therapeutic claims.

Figure 6. Compartment-aware RNA-CyTOF immune-state decoupling map. (A) Whole-blood RNA layer summarizing inflammatory/S100 host-response increase, MHC-II/CD74/HLA-DR decrease and the decoupling index. (B) Paired COMBAT RNA-CyTOF bridge in sepsis-only samples. (C) CyTOF event-derived layer showing residual CD3/CD14 co-event activation-state context and unresolved pair specificity. (D) Claim boundary: the diagram is an association-only, artifact-aware working interpretation requiring prospective validation, not a causal mechanism, clinical validation or structural interaction model.
causal mechanism; validated biomarker; clinical utility
